86c8a6cd4a8c9709.summary: The mean of grouped data can be found using three methods: Direct Method (x̄ = Σfᵢxᵢ/Σfᵢ), Assumed Mean Method (x̄ = a + Σfᵢdᵢ/Σfᵢ), and Step-Deviation Method (x̄ = a + h×Σfᵢuᵢ/Σfᵢ).

chunks.title: Understanding Mean of Grouped Data
chunks.content:
**Mean of Grouped Data** is a key concept in Statistics of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

**Methods for finding mean of grouped data:**

1. **Direct Method:**
   x̄ = Σfᵢxᵢ/Σfᵢ
   where xᵢ = class mark = (upper+lower limit)/2, fᵢ = frequency

2. **Assumed Mean Method:**
   x̄ = a + Σfᵢdᵢ/Σfᵢ
   where dᵢ = xᵢ-a, a = assumed mean

3. **Step-Deviation Method:**
   x̄ = a + h × Σfᵢuᵢ/Σfᵢ
   where uᵢ = (xᵢ-a)/h, h = class width

All three methods give the SAME answer.

**Key points to remember:**
• Class mark = (LCL+UCL)/2
• Σfᵢ = total frequency
• Step-deviation is fastest when classes are equal width
• All methods yield identical mean

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Mean of Grouped Data
chunks.content:
**1. Properties:**
• Direct: Σfx/Σf (no shortcuts)
• Assumed mean: choose a near the middle
• Step-deviation: use when class width h is constant
• Step-deviation simplifies calculations significantly

**2. Important Formulas:**
Direct: x̄ = Σfᵢxᵢ/Σfᵢ
Assumed Mean: x̄ = a + Σfᵢdᵢ/Σfᵢ (dᵢ=xᵢ-a)
Step-Deviation: x̄ = a + h×Σfᵢuᵢ/Σfᵢ (uᵢ=(xᵢ-a)/h)

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
Find mean: Classes 0-10(2), 10-20(5), 20-30(8), 30-40(4).
Direct: x̄ = (5×2+15×5+25×8+35×4)/(2+5+8+4) = (10+75+200+140)/19 = 425/19 = 22.37

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **Wrong class mark:** Class mark = (LCL+UCL)/2, not just the midpoint of one limit

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **Use step-deviation:** Fastest when classes have equal width

✓ **Arrange in table:** Organise data in columns: x, f, u, fu

45e40ea517a89fdc.summary: The mode of grouped data is the class with highest frequency. The exact value is: Mode = l + ((f₁-f₀)/(2f₁-f₀-f₂))×h where l=lower limit, f₁=frequency of modal class, f₀=prev frequency, f₂=next frequency.

chunks.title: Understanding Mode of Grouped Data
chunks.content:
**Mode of Grouped Data** is a key concept in Statistics of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

The **modal class** is the class with the highest frequency.

**Mode formula:**
Mode = l + (f₁-f₀)/(2f₁-f₀-f₂) × h

Where:
• l = lower limit of modal class
• h = class width of modal class
• f₁ = frequency of modal class
• f₀ = frequency of class BEFORE modal class
• f₂ = frequency of class AFTER modal class

**Key points to remember:**
• Modal class = highest frequency class
• f₀ is preceding, f₂ is following frequency
• Mode can be different from class mark
• If f₀=f₂=0 (boundary), adjust carefully

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Mode of Grouped Data
chunks.content:
**1. Properties:**
• Mode is the most frequent value
• Unimodal = one peak; bimodal = two
• Mode formula interpolates within modal class
• Mode is not always at the class mark

**2. Important Formulas:**
Mode = l + (f₁-f₀)/(2f₁-f₀-f₂) × h
l = lower limit of modal class
f₁ = frequency of modal class
f₀ = freq of preceding class
f₂ = freq of following class
h = class width

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
Find mode: 0-10(3), 10-20(8), 20-30(12), 30-40(6), 40-50(2)
Modal class: 20-30. l=20, f₁=12, f₀=8, f₂=6, h=10
Mode = 20 + (12-8)/(24-8-6)×10 = 20 + 4/10×10 = 20+4 = 24

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **Wrong f₀/f₂:** f₀ is BEFORE modal class, f₂ is AFTER

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **Identify modal class first:** Class with highest frequency

d8f781f2d52a3e60.summary: The median of grouped data is the class where the cumulative frequency crosses n/2. Median = l + ((n/2-cf)/f)×h where l is lower limit, cf is cumulative frequency before the median class.

chunks.title: Understanding Median of Grouped Data
chunks.content:
**Median of Grouped Data** is a key concept in Statistics of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

The **median** is the middle value dividing data into two equal halves.

**Steps:**
1. Calculate cumulative frequencies
2. Find n/2
3. Identify median class (where cf crosses n/2)
4. Apply formula:

**Median = l + (n/2 - cf)/f × h**

Where:
• l = lower limit of median class
• n = Σf (total frequency)
• cf = cumulative frequency BEFORE median class
• f = frequency of median class
• h = class width

**Key points to remember:**
• Median divides data: 50% below, 50% above
• n/2 gives the position
• Median class: first class with cf ≥ n/2
• cf = cumulative frequency BEFORE median class

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Median of Grouped Data
chunks.content:
**1. Properties:**
• Less than type ogive: upward slope
• More than type ogive: downward slope
• Median from ogive: draw horizontal at n/2 to curve, then vertical to x-axis
• Median is less affected by outliers than mean

**2. Important Formulas:**
Median = l + (n/2-cf)/f × h
l = lower limit of median class
cf = cumulative freq before median class
f = freq of median class
h = class width
n = Σf

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
Classes: 10-20(5), 20-30(8), 30-40(12), 40-50(10), 50-60(5)
cf: 5, 13, 25, 35, 40. n/2=20.
Median class: 30-40 (cf 13→25).
l=30, cf=13, f=12, h=10.
Median = 30 + (20-13)/12×10 = 30 + 5.83 = 35.83

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **cf vs f confusion:** cf = cumulative freq before median class, not including it

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **cf table first:** Build cf column before finding median class

✓ **n/2 carefully:** Use n/2 to locate the median class

6fd9d91e118f9c0b.summary: Empirical relationship: 3 Median = Mode + 2 Mean. This is used when one of the three measures is unknown. It holds approximately for moderately skewed distributions.

chunks.title: Understanding Empirical Relationship and Ogives
chunks.content:
**Empirical Relationship and Ogives** is a key concept in Statistics of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

**Empirical Relationship:**
3 Median = Mode + 2 Mean

This relationship is approximately true for moderately skewed distributions.

**Ogive (Cumulative Frequency Curve):**
• **Less than ogive:** Plot upper limits vs cumulative frequency
• **More than ogive:** Plot lower limits vs cumulative frequency
• Median is the x-coordinate where ogive intersects y=n/2

**Key points to remember:**
• 3 Median = Mode + 2 Mean
• Use to find Mode when Mean and Median known
• Less than ogive: slopes upward
• Two ogives intersect at median

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Empirical Relationship and Ogives
chunks.content:
**1. Properties:**
• For symmetrical data: Mean=Median=Mode
• Empirical formula is approximate (not exact)
• Ogive helps find median graphically
• Class 10 exams: questions ask median from ogive

**2. Important Formulas:**
3 Median = Mode + 2 Mean
Mean = (3 Median - Mode)/2
Mode = 3 Median - 2 Mean
Median = (Mode + 2 Mean)/3

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
If Median=28 and Mode=24, find Mean.
3×28 = 24+2×Mean
84-24 = 2×Mean
Mean = 30

chunks.title: Worked Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** If Mean=25 and Median=24, find Mode using empirical relationship.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** 3 Median = Mode + 2 Mean
**Step 2:** 3×24 = Mode + 2×25
**Step 3:** 72 = Mode + 50
**Step 4:** Mode = 72-50 = 22

Therefore, Mode = 22.

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **Exact vs approximate:** Empirical formula is approximate, not exact

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **Empirical: 3Med = Mode+2Mean:** Memorize this formula

✓ **Ogive: median by graph:** Draw horizontal line at n/2 to intersection

chunks.title: Practice Problems
chunks.content:
**Problem 1 (Basic):**
Mean=30, Median=28. Find Mode.

**Answer:** Mode = 3×28-2×30 = 84-60 = 24.

---

**Problem 2 (Intermediate):**
Median=35, Mode=29. Find Mean.

**Answer:** Mean = (3×35-29)/2 = (105-29)/2 = 38.

---

**Problem 3 (Advanced):**
From ogive, median is estimated as 34. If mode is 30, estimate mean.

**Answer:** 3×34 = 30+2M → 102-30=2M → M=36.

fc018d283ae9f5fb.summary: Finding mode, median, and mean from grouped data requires identifying the correct class and applying the appropriate formula. Exam problems test all three measures together for a given data set.

chunks.title: Understanding Comprehensive Measures of Central Tendency
chunks.content:
**Comprehensive Measures of Central Tendency** is a key concept in Statistics of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

In board exams, problems often ask to find TWO measures from the same data:
• **Mode + Mean** or **Median + Mean**
• **Mode + Median**
• **All three using empirical relationship**

**Steps for any problem:**
1. Prepare frequency table with class marks
2. Find mean (any method)
3. Build cumulative frequency table
4. Find median (formula)
5. Find mode (or use empirical relation)

**Key points to remember:**
• Organise data in a single table
• Use step-deviation for mean when possible
• Both median and mode need specific formulas
• Verify with empirical relation if plausible

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Comprehensive Measures of Central Tendency
chunks.content:
**1. Properties:**
• Prepare one master table with all columns
• x = class mark, f = frequency, cf = cumulative freq
• d or u columns for mean if step-deviation used
• Check: median lies in modal class or nearby

**2. Important Formulas:**
Mean: x̄ = a + h×Σfu/Σf
Median: l + (n/2-cf)/f × h
Mode: l + (f₁-f₀)/(2f₁-f₀-f₂) × h
Empirical: 3Med = Mode+2Mean

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
Data: 10-20(6), 20-30(10), 30-40(15), 40-50(8), 50-60(5)
x̄=33.2, Median≈33.3, Mode≈33.8
Check: 3(33.3)≈99.9 ≈ 33.8+2(33.2)=100.2 ✓

chunks.title: Worked Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** Find mean and mode:
0-20(5), 20-40(12), 40-60(20), 60-80(8), 80-100(5)

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Mean: classes 10,30,50,70,90. Σfx=50+360+1000+560+450=2420. Σf=50. x̄=48.4
**Step 2:** Modal: 40-60. l=40,f₁=20,f₀=12,f₂=8,h=20
**Step 3:** Mode=40+(20-12)/(40-12-8)×20=40+8/20×20=48

Therefore, Mean = 48.4, Mode = 48.

**Verification:**
Close values suggest symmetric distribution ✓

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **Missing cf for median:** Always build cumulative frequency first

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **Cross-check:** Mean, Median, Mode should be close for symmetric data

d4c47eb41a8ae427.summary: Probability of an event = Number of favourable outcomes / Total number of possible outcomes. Value lies between 0 (impossible) and 1 (certain). Complementary events: P(E) + P(not E) = 1.

chunks.title: Understanding Basic Probability Concepts
chunks.content:
**Basic Probability Concepts** is a key concept in Probability of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

**Probability** is the measure of how likely an event is to occur.

P(E) = Number of favourable outcomes / Total number of possible outcomes

**Key properties:**
• 0 ≤ P(E) ≤ 1
• P(impossible event) = 0
• P(certain event) = 1
• P(E) + P(not E) = 1 (complementary events)
• Sum of probabilities of all outcomes = 1

**Random experiment:** An experiment whose outcome cannot be predicted in advance (e.g., coin toss, dice roll).

**Key points to remember:**
• Probabilities are always between 0 and 1
• P(E) + P(not E) = 1
• All outcomes equally likely in simple problems
• Favourable outcomes ≤ Total outcomes

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Basic Probability Concepts
chunks.content:
**1. Properties:**
• Sample space: set of all possible outcomes
• Event: subset of sample space
• Equally likely outcomes: each has same chance
• Complement: not E = everything except E

**2. Important Formulas:**
P(E) = fav/total
0 ≤ P(E) ≤ 1
P(E) + P(not E) = 1
ΣP(all outcomes) = 1
P(certain) = 1, P(impossible) = 0

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
Toss a coin. P(getting head) = 1/2 = 0.5.
Roll a die. P(getting 4) = 1/6.
P(not 4) = 1-1/6 = 5/6.

chunks.title: Worked Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** A bag has 3 red, 5 blue, 2 green balls. Find P(red) and P(not blue).

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Total balls = 3+5+2 = 10
**Step 2:** P(red) = 3/10 = 0.3
**Step 3:** P(not blue) = 1 - P(blue) = 1-5/10 = 5/10 = 0.5

Therefore, P(red) = 0.3, P(not blue) = 0.5.

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **P > 1 or P < 0:** Probability is always between 0 and 1

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **P(not E) = 1-P(E):** Use complements to simplify

✓ **Fav/total:** Always: favourable / total possible

chunks.title: Practice Problems
chunks.content:
**Problem 1 (Basic):**
Toss 2 coins. Find P(2 heads).

**Answer:** Total=4 (HH,HT,TH,TT). Fav=1 (HH). P=1/4.

---

**Problem 2 (Intermediate):**
A die rolled. Find P(even).

**Answer:** Even: 2,4,6. Fav=3. Total=6. P=1/2.

---

**Problem 3 (Advanced):**
Bag: 4 white, 6 black, 5 red. Find P(not black).

**Answer:** Total=15. Not black=9. P=9/15=3/5.

27b119be60dec30a.summary: For compound events (two coins, two dice, cards, etc.), we list the sample space systematically. Number of outcomes for n coins = 2ⁿ. Number of outcomes for n dice = 6ⁿ.

chunks.title: Understanding Compound Events
chunks.content:
**Compound Events** is a key concept in Probability of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

**Compound events** involve two or more simple events.

**Number of possible outcomes:**
• 1 coin: 2 outcomes (H,T)
• 2 coins: 4 outcomes (HH,HT,TH,TT)
• 3 coins: 8 outcomes
• 1 die: 6 outcomes
• 2 dice: 36 outcomes

**Cards (standard deck):** 52 cards
• 4 suits: Spades(♠), Hearts(♥), Diamonds(♦), Clubs(♣)
• Each suit: 13 cards (A,2-10,J,Q,K)
• Face cards: J, Q, K (3 per suit = 12 total)

**Key points to remember:**
• 2 coins: 4 outcomes
• 2 dice: 36 outcomes
• 52 cards: 13 per suit
• Face cards: J,Q,K = 12 total

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Compound Events
chunks.content:
**1. Properties:**
• Two dice sum ranges from 2 to 12
• Sum=7 has maximum probability (6/36=1/6)
• P(face card)=12/52=3/13
• P(ace)=4/52=1/13
• P(red card)=26/52=1/2

**2. Important Formulas:**
n coins: 2ⁿ outcomes
n dice: 6ⁿ outcomes
P(at least 1 head) = 1-P(no heads)
P(king) = 4/52 = 1/13
P(red king) = 2/52 = 1/26

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
Two dice: P(sum=7) = 6/36 = 1/6.
Favourable: (1,6),(2,5),(3,4),(4,3),(5,2),(6,1)

chunks.title: Worked Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** From a well-shuffled deck of 52 cards, find P(a king) and P(red face card).

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** P(king) = 4/52 = 1/13
**Step 2:** Red face cards: Hearts: J,Q,K; Diamonds: J,Q,K = 6 cards
**Step 3:** P(red face card) = 6/52 = 3/26

Therefore, P(king) = 1/13, P(red face card) = 3/26.

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **Face cards include Aces?:** No. Face cards = J,Q,K only

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **Sample space first:** List all possible outcomes before counting

chunks.title: Practice Problems
chunks.content:
**Problem 1 (Basic):**
Two dice: P(sum=8).

**Answer:** (2,6),(3,5),(4,4),(5,3),(6,2)=5 ways. P=5/36.

---

**Problem 2 (Intermediate):**
Deck: P(black king).

**Answer:** Black kings: Spade K, Club K = 2. P=2/52=1/26.

---

**Problem 3 (Advanced):**
3 coins: P(at least 2 heads).

**Answer:** Total=8. At least 2H: HHT,HTH,THH,HHH=4. P=1/2.

657a672eb0feb0b5.summary: Problems involving 'at least one', 'at most', 'neither...nor', and 'either...or' use complementary events and set theory. P(at least one) = 1-P(none).

chunks.title: Understanding Event Relationships: At Least, At Most, Neither
chunks.content:
**Event Relationships: At Least, At Most, Neither** is a key concept in Probability of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

**Key relationships:**
• P(at least one) = 1 - P(none)
• P(at most one) = P(0) + P(1)
• P(neither A nor B) = 1 - P(A or B)
• P(exactly one) = P(A but not B) + P(B but not A)

**For 'at least one' problems:**
These are easier to solve by finding the complement (none) and subtracting from 1.

**Key points to remember:**
• 'At least one' = complement of 'none'
• 'At most two' = P(0)+P(1)+P(2)
• 'Neither' = complement of 'either'
• Use Venn diagrams for complex events

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Event Relationships: At Least, At Most, Neither
chunks.content:
**1. Properties:**
• P(at least 1 head in 3 coins) = 1-P(no heads)=1-1/8=7/8
• P(at most 1 head in 3 coins) = P(0H)+P(1H)=1/8+3/8=1/2
• P(neither king nor ace) = 1-P(king or ace)
• For non-mutually exclusive: P(A or B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A and B)

**2. Important Formulas:**
P(at least one) = 1 - P(none)
P(at most one) = P(0) + P(1)
P(neither A nor B) = 1 - P(A∪B)
P(A or B) = P(A)+P(B)-P(A∩B)

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
Toss 3 coins: P(at least 1 tail) = 1-P(no tails)=1-1/8=7/8.

chunks.title: Worked Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** Two dice: find P(at least one 6).

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Total outcomes = 36
**Step 2:** P(no six) = 5×5/36 = 25/36 (each die not 6: 5 ways each)
**Step 3:** P(at least one 6) = 1-25/36 = 11/36

Therefore, P(at least one 6) = 11/36.

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **Double-counting:** When using P(A)+P(B), subtract P(A∩B)

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **'At least' → complement:** Use 1-P(none) — much simpler

chunks.title: Practice Problems
chunks.content:
**Problem 1 (Basic):**
Deck: P(neither heart nor king).

**Answer:** P(heart or king)=13/52+3/52=16/52. P(neither)=36/52=9/13.

---

**Problem 2 (Intermediate):**
Two dice: P(at least one odd).

**Answer:** P(both even)=9/36=1/4. P(at least one odd)=1-1/4=3/4.

---

**Problem 3 (Advanced):**
3 coins: P(at most one head).

**Answer:** P(0H)+P(1H)=1/8+3/8=1/2.

9a3bc36e357554cd.summary: Applications of probability in real-life contexts: birth dates, defective items, sports, weather, and quality control. Focus on defining outcomes correctly in real-world scenarios.

chunks.title: Understanding Real-World Applications of Probability
chunks.content:
**Real-World Applications of Probability** is a key concept in Probability of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

Probability has many real-world applications:
• **Quality control:** Defect rate in manufactured items
• **Weather forecasting:** Chance of rain = 70%
• **Insurance:** Risk assessment and premium calculation
• **Genetics:** Inheritance probability
• **Sports:** Win/loss prediction

**Key skill:** Convert word problems into probability scenarios by correctly identifying favourable and total outcomes.

**Key points to remember:**
• Check if outcomes are equally likely
• Look for symmetry or past data
• Use complement for 'at least' problems
• Verify probability is between 0 and 1

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Real-World Applications of Probability
chunks.content:
**1. Properties:**
• Defect rate: P(defective)=defective/total
• Two children: 4 outcomes (BB,BG,GB,GG)
• Birthday: P(same birthday) = 1/365 (simplified)
• Rain probability: based on historical data

**2. Important Formulas:**
P(defective) = n_defective / n_total
P(same birthday) ≈ 1/365
P(at least one boy in 2 kids) = 1-P(2 girls)=1-1/4=3/4

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
From 50 items with 5 defective, P(non-defective) = 45/50 = 9/10.

chunks.title: Worked Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** A family has 2 children. Find P(at least one boy).

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Sample space: BB, BG, GB, GG (4 outcomes)
**Step 2:** P(no boy) = P(GG) = 1/4
**Step 3:** P(at least 1 boy) = 1-1/4 = 3/4

Therefore, P(at least one boy) = 3/4.

**Verification:**
Assuming equal probability of B and G.

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **Assuming equally likely:** Check if outcomes are truly equally likely

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **List outcomes:** Write down all possibilities before calculating

chunks.title: Practice Problems
chunks.content:
**Problem 1 (Basic):**
From 100 items with 8 defective, find P(good).

**Answer:** Good=92. P=92/100=23/25.

---

**Problem 2 (Intermediate):**
Two children: P(exactly one girl).

**Answer:** BG,GB=2 ways. Total=4. P=1/2.

---

**Problem 3 (Advanced):**
In a class of 30, 18 girls. Find P(selected student is boy).

**Answer:** Boys=12. P=12/30=2/5.
